levers.name: suite_layout
levers.consequences: Determines participant comfort and naturalistic behavior, potentially affecting data quality.
options: Compact shared bedrooms with enhanced video monitoring
Private suites with reduced camera presence
Suite clusters with a mix of private and shared spaces
levers.lever_classification: methodology — which participant environment to create

levers.name: sensing_model
levers.consequences: Impacts data resolution, false alarm rates, and annotation throughput.
options: Tiered acquisition model with low-burden monitoring and enhanced nights
Full PSG on all participant nights
Event-driven deployment of full PSG for flagged events
levers.lever_classification: methodology — how to collect data

levers.name: recruitment_channels
levers.consequences: Affects participant diversity, yield rates, and study efficiency.
options: University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic and regional neurologist referrals
Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network and online advertising
Direct outreach to local sleep disorder support groups
levers.lever_classification: operations — how to find participants

levers.name: participant_compensation
levers.consequences: Influences participant engagement, retention, and yield rates.
options: €80 per night plus meals
€100 per night plus meals
€120 per night with additional incentives for longer stays
levers.lever_classification: operations — how to incentivize participants

levers.name: event-triage_tool_development
levers.consequences: Impacts manual review burden, data quality, and study efficiency.
options: Development of semi-automated tools with dual human scorer agreement
Use of machine learning algorithms for event prioritization
Manual review only with no automated triage
levers.lever_classification: methodology — how to analyze data

levers.name: participant_screening_process
levers.consequences: Affects participant selection, yield rates, and study validity.
options: Structured pre-admission pathway with clinical interview and collateral history
Inclusion of additional screening tools for sleep disorders
Simplified participant selection process with reduced screening
levers.lever_classification: operations — how to select participants

levers.name: data_sharing_protocol
levers.consequences: Impacts data accessibility, reuse, and study impact.
options: De-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements
Public deposition of annotated EEG-BIDS-compatible format data
Data sharing restricted to study collaborators only
levers.lever_classification: dissemination — how to share results

levers.name: suite_expansion_strategy
levers.consequences: Deciding on how to expand the facility's physical capacity will directly impact staffing ratios, data quality, and participant recruitment. If expansion is too aggressive, it may compromise the pilot study's integrity.
options: Expand the existing property by converting adjacent residential units for additional sleep suites
Acquire a new nearby property to consolidate research activities under one roof
Implement a phased expansion plan, renovating and equipping suites in stages
levers.review_lever: The options all assume that expanding capacity will directly correlate with increased participant recruitment, but the relationship between suite numbers and admissions is not linear. A deeper analysis of historical data or a controlled experiment would be needed to accurately model this relationship.
levers.lever_classification: operations — how to expand the facility's physical capacity

levers.name: technician_training_program
levers.consequences: Deciding on how to train research technicians will directly impact data quality, participant safety, and staff burnout. If training is inadequate, it may lead to errors in data annotation or participant care.
options: Develop a comprehensive, standardized training program with regular assessments and certifications
Implement an apprenticeship model where senior technicians mentor junior staff
Provide on-the-job training with minimal formal instruction
levers.review_lever: The options all assume that technician training is a one-time event, but research has shown that continuous learning and skill-building are essential in high-stakes environments like this facility.
levers.lever_classification: execution — how to train research technicians

levers.name: participant_retention_tactics
levers.consequences: Deciding on how to retain participants will directly impact data quality, participant satisfaction, and study completion rates. If retention strategies are inadequate, it may lead to high dropout rates or biased data.
options: Implement a personalized engagement program with tailored incentives for each participant
Develop a comprehensive survey and feedback system to monitor participant satisfaction
Offer flexible scheduling and compensation packages to accommodate participants' needs
levers.review_lever: The options all assume that participant retention is solely dependent on extrinsic factors, but research has shown that intrinsic motivators like sense of purpose or personal growth can be just as effective.
levers.lever_classification: governance — how to retain participants in the study

levers.name: data_sharing_policies
levers.consequences: Deciding on data sharing policies will directly impact participant privacy, research collaboration, and scientific reproducibility. If policies are too restrictive, it may limit opportunities for cross-disciplinary collaboration or data reuse.
options: Implement a strict, opt-in data-sharing policy with minimal metadata release
Develop a tiered data-sharing model with varying levels of access based on research proposals
Adopt an open-data policy with unrestricted sharing and reuse
levers.review_lever: The options all assume that data sharing is solely dependent on technical infrastructure, but research has shown that social and cultural contexts play a significant role in shaping data-sharing practices.
levers.lever_classification: dissemination — how to share data from the study with other researchers or organizations

levers.name: research_collaboration_model
levers.consequences: Deciding on a research collaboration model will directly impact knowledge sharing, resource allocation, and scientific impact. If collaborations are too loose, it may lead to duplicated efforts or missed opportunities.
options: Develop a formal partnership with the University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology
Establish a collaborative network with external research institutions and organizations
Implement a hub-and-spoke model with centralized data management and decentralized analysis
levers.review_lever: The options all assume that research collaborations are solely dependent on institutional relationships, but research has shown that informal networks and personal connections can be just as effective.
levers.lever_classification: governance — how to collaborate with other researchers or organizations in the study

levers.name: participant_compensation_model
levers.consequences: Deciding on a participant compensation model will directly impact recruitment rates, participant satisfaction, and data quality. If compensation is inadequate, it may lead to biased or incomplete data.
options: Offer a flat rate of €80 per night with no additional incentives
Implement a tiered compensation system based on participant engagement and contributions
Develop a personalized compensation package tailored to each participant's needs
levers.review_lever: The options all assume that participant compensation is solely dependent on extrinsic factors, but research has shown that intrinsic motivators like sense of purpose or personal growth can be just as effective.
levers.lever_classification: product — how to compensate participants for their participation in the study

levers.name: suite_configuration
levers.consequences: Configuring suites for optimal participant comfort and safety can lead to higher participant retention rates, but may compromise data quality if participants become too comfortable.
options: Configure suites with adjustable lighting and temperature controls
Use soundproofing materials to minimize external noise
Install smart home automation systems for enhanced comfort
levers.lever_classification: operations — how to configure the residential research unit's living spaces for optimal participant experience and data quality

levers.name: participant_screening_processes
levers.consequences: Implementing rigorous screening processes can reduce the risk of including participants with uncontrolled medical conditions, but may lead to delays in participant recruitment.
options: Use a comprehensive clinical interview and collateral history from bed partners or household members
Require prior polysomnography results for all participants
Conduct on-site neurological screenings for suspected epilepsy mimics
levers.lever_classification: governance — what criteria to use for participant selection and exclusion

levers.name: data_sharing_policies
levers.consequences: Sharing de-identified physiological data can facilitate collaboration and advance scientific knowledge, but may compromise participant privacy.
options: Deposit all bedroom video in public repositories
Share only aggregated and anonymized data with external researchers
Limit data sharing to internal research partners only
levers.lever_classification: governance — what policies to implement for sharing participant data

levers.name: technician_training_programs
levers.consequences: Investing in comprehensive technician training can enhance data quality and reduce annotation burdens, but may divert resources away from other critical areas.
options: Develop a multi-day training program for new technicians
Provide ongoing workshops and peer mentoring for experienced technicians
Offer specialized training for technicians working with sensitive patient populations
levers.lever_classification: operations — how to train research staff in data collection and annotation

levers.name: recruitment_channel_mixtures
levers.consequences: Diversifying recruitment channels can increase participant enrollment rates, but may lead to higher costs and logistical complexities.
options: Focus on a single primary recruitment channel (e.g., University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic)
Use a mix of clinical referrals, online advertising, and community outreach programs
Leverage social media platforms and online forums for participant recruitment
levers.lever_classification: operations — how to recruit participants for the residential research unit

levers.name: participant_compensation_models
levers.consequences: Implementing generous participant compensation models can enhance participant retention rates, but may compromise data quality if participants become too focused on financial rewards.
options: Offer a standard per-night compensation of €80 plus meals
Provide additional incentives for participants who complete extended stays or produce high-quality data
Use a tiered compensation model that rewards participants for achieving specific research milestones
levers.lever_classification: governance — what policies to implement for participant compensation

levers.name: data_sharing_pact_negotiations
levers.consequences: Negotiating data sharing agreements with external partners can facilitate collaboration and advance scientific knowledge, but may compromise participant privacy.
options: Require all external partners to sign a comprehensive data use agreement
Establish a tiered access model that restricts sensitive data to authorized researchers only
Use a 'data-sharing-by-default' approach that prioritizes openness and transparency
levers.lever_classification: governance — what policies to implement for sharing participant data with external partners

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Classification**
   - `lever_classification`: a brief phrase with the category and what the lever decides.
   - Format: "category — what this lever decides".
   - Categories: methodology, execution, governance, dissemination, product, operations.
   - Examples: "governance — who oversees the review process", "methodology — which data collection approach to use", "execution — how to sequence the rollout phases".

3. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

4. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

5. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but none of the options price in the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season."
     - "Routing the light-rail extension through the historic district unlocks ridership but triggers Section 106 heritage review; the options assume permits will clear on the standard timeline."
     - "Pooling catastrophe risk across three coastal regions diversifies exposure on paper, but a regional hurricane season can correlate all three simultaneously — correlation risk absent from every option."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

6. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

7. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.

Today's date:
2026-Mar-11

Project start ASAP

File 'purpose.md':
**Purpose:** business

**Purpose Detailed:** Research facility establishment, data collection, and analysis for scientific publication and grant funding.

**Topic:** Establishment of a residential research unit for the study of adult NREM parasomnias

File 'plan_type.md':
This plan requires one or more physical locations. It cannot be executed digitally.

**Explanation:** This plan *unequivocally requires* a physical location (the research unit in Bonn, Germany), physical renovations, physical equipment, in-person staffing, and physical participants. The entire research project *revolves around* physical data collection and analysis in a real-world setting. The plan also requires physical travel to the location.
